2: lo-tosefu al-hadavar asher anochi metzaveh etchem velo tigre'u mimenu lishmor et-mitzvot YHWH Eloheichem asher anochi metzaveh etchem (lōʾ tōsipû ʿal-haddābār ʾăšer ʾānōkî mĕṣawweh ʾetkkem wĕlōʾ tigĕrĕʾû mimmennû lišmōr ʾet-miṣwōt Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem ʾăšer ʾānōkî mĕṣawweh ʾetkkem): 'You shall not add to the word that I command you, nor take from it, that you may keep the commandments of the LORD your God that I command you.' The double prohibition — neither addition (tōsipû) nor subtraction (tigĕrĕʾû) — defines the canonical boundary of Torah. The same formula reappears in Deuteronomy 12:32 and in Revelation's closing warning (Deuteronomy 22:18-19), which applies it to the entirety of Scripture. The principle is that the covenant document is complete and authoritative as given; human modification in either direction distorts the covenant relationship it mediates.
12: vayedabber YHWH aleichem mitoch ha'esh kol devarim atem shomim utemunah einekhem ro'im zulati kol (waydabbēr Yhwh ʾălêkem mittôk hāʾēš qôl dĕbārîm ʾattem šōmĕʿîm ûtĕmûnāh ʾênkem rōʾîm zûlātî qôl): 'Then the LORD spoke to you out of the midst of the fire. You heard the sound of words, but saw no form; there was only a voice.' The programmatic invisibility of YHWH at Horeb is Deuteronomy's key theological safeguard against idolatry: Israel heard kol devarim (sound/voice of words) but saw temunah (form, likeness) of none. The noun temunah is the same word Moses uses of himself in Numbers 12:8 (Moses sees the temunah of YHWH — a special prophetic privilege that proves the rule of hiddenness). The invisibility grounds the second commandment: since YHWH showed no visible form, any representation is a falsification. John 1:18 ('no one has ever seen God') and John 5:37 ('his form you have never seen') stand in direct continuity with this Deuteronomic tradition.
24: ki YHWH Eloheicha esh okhlah hu El kanna (kî Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ʾēš ʾōklāh hûʾ ʾēl qannāʾ): 'For the LORD your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God.' Esh okhlah (consuming fire) — fire that devours completely, leaving nothing. The image combines theophanic fire (Horeb), divine holiness, and judicial wrath. El qanna — the root qn' (jealousy/zeal) describes YHWH's exclusive claim on Israel's allegiance, corresponding to the marriage metaphor of covenant: YHWH as Israel's husband tolerates no rivals (Exodus 20:5; Deuteronomy 34:14). Hebrews 12:29 cites this verse directly in its climactic warning: 'for our God is a consuming fire' — the God of the new covenant is no less holy, his covenant-demands no less total, than at Sinai.
35: Atah hor'eta lada'at ki YHWH hu ha-Elohim ein od milvado (ʾAttāh horrĕtā lādaʿat kî Yhwh hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm ʾên ʿôd milvaddô): 'To you it was shown, that you might know that the LORD is God; there is no other besides him.' Ein od milvado — there is nothing else apart from him. This is Deuteronomy's foundational monotheistic formula, repeated in Deuteronomy 4:39 and echoed throughout Second Isaiah (Isaiah 45:5,6,14,18,21,22; Deuteronomy 46:9). The Horeb revelation — fire, darkness, cloud, and the voice of words — was given precisely to produce cognitive certainty (lādaʿat, to know) of YHWH's sole deity. The NT applies the same epistemological structure to Christ: the incarnation as the definitive showing (horrĕtā) that enables knowledge of God (John 1:18: the only God who is at the Father's side — he has made him known).
1: ve'attah Yisra'el shema el-hahuqqim ve'el-hamishpatim asher anokhi melammedkem: 'And now, O Israel, listen to the statutes and rules I am teaching you.' The ve'attah (and now) construction is a rhetorical transition — Moses pivots from historical recitation (Deuteronomy 1-3) to direct address and instruction. Shema (hear, listen) — the imperative that gives the Shema its name; attentive listening is the covenantal posture. Huqqim umishpatim (statutes and rules): the huqqim are the fixed divine ordinances whose rationale is not always explained (e.g., food laws, calendar); the mishpatim are case-law decisions whose legal logic is transparent. Together they constitute the comprehensive Torah-deposit.
3: eineikhem haro'ot et asher-asah YHWH beBa'al Pe'or ki kol-ha'ish asher halakh aharei Ba'al-Pe'or hishmido YHWH Eloheicha miqirbekha: 'Your eyes have seen what the LORD did at Baal-peor — the LORD your God destroyed from among you every man who followed Baal-peor.' The appeal to eyewitness memory (eineikhem haro'ot) is Moses's primary rhetorical tool: the present generation saw the Baal-peor judgment (Num 25) with their own eyes. The destruction was precise — kol-ha'ish asher halakh aharei Ba'al-Pe'or (every man who followed Baal-peor), the guilty without exception. The survivors reading Deuteronomy are living proof of YHWH's distinction between the faithful and the apostate.
4: ve'atem hadeveqim beYHWH Eloheichem hayyim kulchem hayom: 'But you who held fast to the LORD your God are all alive today.' Hadeveqim — the participle of davaq (to cling, cleave, stick fast) — is Deuteronomy's word for covenantal fidelity. The same verb describes the marriage bond (Genesis 2:24: 'he shall cleave to his wife'), Ruth's commitment to Naomi (Ruth 1:14), and the devotion demanded in 10:20 and Deuteronomy 11:22 ('hold fast to him'). NT usage: 'hold fast to what is good' (Romans 12:9), 'holding fast to the head' (Colossians 2:19). The contrast with Deuteronomy 4:3 is stark: those who clung to Baal died; those who clung to YHWH live. Life flows from covenant fidelity.
5: re'eh limmadeti etchem huqqim umishpatim ka'asher tzivvani YHWH Elohai la'asot ken beqerev ha'aretz asher atem ba'im shamma leishtah: 'See, I have taught you statutes and rules, as the LORD my God commanded me, so that you should do them in the land you are entering.' Moses's authority is derivative: ka'asher tzivvani YHWH Elohai (as the LORD my God commanded me). He is a transmitter, not an originator, of Torah. This mediatorial role is precisely the role the NT applies to Jesus as the greater Moses: he too is sent (John 7:16: 'my teaching is not mine but his who sent me') — but with a crucial difference: Jesus speaks on his own authority ('You have heard... but I say to you,' Matthew 5:21-48), not merely relaying another's word.
6: ushmartem va'asitem ki hi chokhmatchem uvinatchem le'einei ha'ammim asher yishme'un et-kol-hahuqqim ha'eleh: 'Keep and carry out these statutes, for this will be your wisdom and understanding before the other peoples.' Torah observance as witness to the nations — this is Deuteronomy's missional vision. Chokhmatchem uvinatchem (your wisdom and understanding) — Israel's distinctiveness is intellectual and moral as well as religious: the Torah reveals a way of living that is coherent, just, and ordered. The rhetorical question follows: 'What great nation has statutes as righteous as all this law?' (Deuteronomy 4:8). The implied answer positions Israel as a model society that draws the nations to inquiry about YHWH.
7: ki mi-goy gadol asher-lo Elohim qerovim elav kaYHWH Eloheinu bekhol-qor'enu elav: 'For what great nation has a god as close to it as the LORD our God is to us whenever we call upon him?' Qerovim elav — near to it/him. YHWH's accessibility is one of two distinguishing marks of Israel's God (the other being the righteous Torah, Deuteronomy 4:8). Accessibility on demand (bekhol-qor'enu elav, in all our calling to him) distinguishes YHWH from the distant, unpredictable gods of Mesopotamia. The NT applies the same accessibility to Christ as the mediator who gives believers direct access to the Father (Hebrews 4:16: 'approach the throne of grace with confidence'; Romans 8:15-16: Abba — Father).
8: umi goy gadol asher-lo huqqim umishpatim tzaddiqim kekhol-hatorah hazot asher anokhi noten lifneikhem hayom: 'What great nation has statutes and rules as righteous as this entire law I am setting before you today?' The comparative claim for Torah's righteousness (tzaddiqim) is bold: no other legal corpus matches the ethical quality of YHWH's covenant law. In Paul's framing (Romans 7:12: 'the law is holy, righteous, and good') this uniqueness of Torah is affirmed even as Paul argues for the law's limited ability to produce the righteousness it demands — a limitation that Christ's spirit fulfills (Romans 8:4).
9: raq hishamer lekha ushemor nafshekha me'od pen-tishkah et-hadevarim asher-ra'u einekha: 'Only be careful, and guard yourselves diligently, lest you forget the things your eyes have seen.' The double imperative — hishamer (guard yourself) and shemor nafshekha (guard your soul/life) — and the modifier me'od (very, diligently) signal the seriousness of memory as a covenant obligation. Pen-tishkah (lest you forget) — the danger of forgetting is not merely cognitive but covenantal: to forget YHWH's acts is to lose the basis for covenant loyalty. Deuteronomy's entire structure as a repeated recitation of history serves this anti-forgetting purpose.
10: yom asher amadta lifnei YHWH Eloheicha beHorev be'amor YHWH elai haqhel-li et-ha'am ve'ashmi'em et-devarai: 'Tell them about the day you stood before the LORD your God at Horeb, when the LORD said to me: Gather the people to me and I will let them hear my words.' The Horeb assembly (yom asher amadta lifnei YHWH) is the constitutive event for all subsequent generations. Haqhel-li et-ha'am (gather the people to me) — the assembly before YHWH is a paradigmatic act that the NT picks up: Jesus gathers his disciples to deliver the greater Torah in the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:1-2), explicitly evoking the Sinai/Horeb gathering.
11: vatiqrevu vata'amdu tahat hahar vehahar bo'er va'esh ad-lev hashamayim choshekh anan va'arafel: 'And you came near and stood at the foot of the mountain while the mountain burned with fire up to the heart of heaven — darkness, cloud, and thick darkness.' The triple darkness vocabulary — choshekh (darkness), anan (cloud), arafel (thick cloud/darkness) — marks the Horeb theophany as both terrifying and bounded. YHWH is present but hidden; audible but not visible. The same combination (cloud + fire + voice) structures the NT transfiguration (Matthew 17:5: 'a bright cloud overshadowed them, and a voice from the cloud said...') — the Son's identity disclosed where Moses's identity was established.
13: vayagged lachem et-berito asher tzivah etchem la'asot aseret hadevarim vayikhtevem al-shney luhot avanim: 'He declared to you his covenant that he commanded you to keep — the Ten Commandments — and he wrote them on two stone tablets.' Aseret hadevarim (the ten words) — not 'commandments' but 'words' (devarim), the same term for Deuteronomy's title (the 'words' of Moses). The Decalogue is the covenant treaty-document written by YHWH himself on stone (luhot avanim): both the durable medium and the divine author distinguish it from all other law. Paul's contrast in 2 Corinthians 3:3 (not on stone tablets but on tablets of human hearts) invokes this distinction: the new covenant internalization of Torah surpasses the externally written Decalogue.
14: ve'oti tzivvah YHWH baat hahi lelammed etchem huqqim umishpatim: 'And the LORD commanded me at that time to teach you statutes and rules for you to carry out in the land you are entering to possess.' Moses's teaching role is here explicitly commanded, not self-appointed. Lelammed (to teach) — the Piel of lamad, the intensive teaching verb from which Talmud derives. Moses is the first Torah-teacher, and his model establishes the Deuteronomic-prophetic tradition of instruction that culminates in the Teacher who is greater than Moses (Matthew 23:10: 'you have one teacher, the Christ').
15: venishmartem me'od lenafshoteichem ki lo re'item kol temunah beyom dibber YHWH aleichem beHorev mittokh ha'esh: 'Therefore watch yourselves very carefully. Since you saw no form on the day the LORD spoke to you at Horeb out of the midst of the fire.' The explicit grounding of the second commandment (no images, Deuteronomy 4:16-18) in the invisibility of Horeb is Deuteronomy's unique contribution to Israelite anti-idol theology. Lo re'item kol temunah (you saw no form at all) — the totalizing negation (kol) rules out any partial or symbolic representation. YHWH's hiddenness is permanent, not temporary; the command against images is therefore permanent.
16: pen-tashkitun va'asitem lachem pesel temunat kol-semel tavnit zakhar o neqevah: 'Do not act corruptly by making for yourselves a carved image in the form of any figure — the likeness of male or female.' Tashkitun — the root shahat (to destroy, corrupt) — making an image is destroying one's relationship with YHWH. The enumeration of prohibited image-types (human, animal, bird, fish, heavenly bodies — Deuteronomy 4:16-19) is exhaustive: every category of created being that ancient Near Eastern cultures worshipped is explicitly forbidden. The prohibition is not aesthetic but covenantal: to represent YHWH as any creature is to misidentify him fundamentally.
17: tavnit kol-behemah asher baaretz tavnit kol-tzippor kanaf asher ta'uf bashamayim: 'The likeness of any animal on the earth, the likeness of any winged bird that flies in the sky.' The anti-image catalog continues. Egypt's theriomorphic gods — bull-headed, falcon-headed, crocodile-headed deities — are precisely the image-forms prohibited here. Israel came out of a culture saturated with animal-deity iconography; the second commandment is a counter-cultural demand rooted in YHWH's transcendence. The golden calf incident (Exod 32) — occurring immediately after the Decalogue — shows how deep the pull toward animal imagery ran in Israel.
18: tavnit kol-romes baaadamah tavnit kol-dagah asher-bamayim mitahat laaretz: 'The likeness of anything that creeps on the ground, the likeness of any fish in the water beneath the earth.' The four-zone enumeration (human/animal, bird, creeping thing, fish) corresponds to the Genesis 1 creation account's four creature categories. The anti-image prohibition thus maps onto creation itself: every domain of the created order has been worshipped by humans; none represents YHWH. The comprehensive prohibition is implicitly a confession of creation theology: YHWH is the Creator of all categories and is therefore represented by none of them.
19: upen-tissa einekha hashamayima uva'ita et-hashemesh ve'et-hayareaah ve'et-hakokhavim kol-tzva hashamayim venidahta vehishtahavita lahem uvedita: 'Beware lest you lift your eyes to heaven and see the sun and moon and stars — all the host of heaven — and be drawn away to bow down and serve them.' Tzva hashamayim (host of heaven) — the astral pantheon of Babylonian and Canaanite religion, which Israel repeatedly adopted (2 Kings 17:16; Deuteronomy 21:3; Jeremiah 19:13). Venidahta (and be drawn away) — the root nadah means to be thrust aside, led astray by attraction. The danger is visual: the beauty of celestial objects becomes a spiritual enticement. Deuteronomy's warning predicts precisely the apostasy of the divided monarchy.
20: ve'etchem laqah YHWH vayotzeh etchem mikkur habarzel miMitzrayim: 'But the LORD has taken you and brought you out of the iron furnace, out of Egypt.' Kur habarzel (iron furnace) — the smelting furnace is an image of intense, refining affliction. Egypt as iron furnace appears also in 1 Kings 8:51 (Solomon's prayer) and Jeremiah 11:4. The metallurgical image implies that Israel's suffering in Egypt was not accidental but purposeful: YHWH was forging a people. La'am nachala (to be a people of inheritance) — Israel is YHWH's nachala (inheritance, treasured possession), the very term used in Psalms 2:8 of the nations given to the Messiah as inheritance.
21: veYHWH hit'anef-bi al-divreichem vayishava'a asher-lo e'evor et-haYarden: 'Furthermore, the LORD was angry with me on your account and swore that I would not cross the Jordan.' Hit'anef — the Hithpael of anaph (to be angry) means to become intensely angry against oneself's interlocutor. The use of the reflexive-intensive form intensifies the anger. Vayishava (and he swore) — YHWH's anger becomes an oath: Moses's exclusion is now irrevocable, sealed by divine vow. The phrase al-divreichem (on account of your words/matters) explicitly places the blame on Israel's corporate sin — Moses is punished representatively, bearing consequences for the people's faithlessness.
22: ki anokhi met baaretz hazot eini over et-haYarden ve'atem overim virishatem et-ha'aretz hatovah hazot: 'I must die in this land; I am not to cross the Jordan. But you shall cross over and possess that good land.' The contrast is stark and deliberate: anokhi met (I die) / ve'atem overim virishatem (you shall cross and possess). Moses's death enables Israel's inheritance — a pattern that the NT applies to the death of Christ: 'unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone, but if it dies, it bears much fruit' (John 12:24). The leader who cannot enter makes room for the one who can bring them in.
23: hishamru lachem pen-tishkehu et-berit YHWH Eloheichem asher karat imachem: 'Be careful not to forget the covenant of the LORD your God that he made with you.' The covenant (berit) is the object of potential forgetting — not merely a law or a command, but a relationship. Pen-tishkehu (lest you forget) — the very thing that makes idolatry possible is covenant amnesia: when YHWH's acts and character slip from memory, the vacuum is filled by attractive false alternatives. The entire rhetoric of Deuteronomy is designed to prevent this forgetting through repeated recitation, ceremony, and written inscription.
25: ki tolid banim uvnei vanim venoshantem baaretz vehishkatem va'asitem pesel temunat kol va'asitem hara be'einei YHWH Eloheicha lehakh'iso: 'When you have children and grandchildren and have grown old in the land, and if you act corruptly by making a carved image in the form of anything — doing what is evil in the eyes of the LORD your God so as to provoke him.' The prediction of future apostasy frames it as a covenantal inevitability rooted in human nature: prosperity and generational distance from founding events breed forgetfulness and corruption. Lehakh'iso (to provoke him to anger) — the root ka'as (to be angry, to be provoked) is the covenantal term for Israel's persistent rebellion as a domestic provocation against YHWH.
26: ha'idoti vachem hayom et-hashamayim ve'et-ha'aretz ki avod tovdun maher me'al haaretz asher atem overim et-haYarden shammah lerishta: 'I call heaven and earth as witnesses against you today that you will quickly perish from the land.' Ha'idoti vachem et-hashamayim ve'et-haaretz — the treaty-curse invocation of cosmic witnesses is standard suzerainty-treaty form: heaven and earth are summoned to testify to the covenant's terms and to observe any violation. The same invocation opens the covenant litigation in Deuteronomy 30:19 and Isaiah 1:2, where YHWH sues Israel before the cosmic court. The witnesses of heaven and earth give the covenant a universal, public character: Israel's faithlessness is not a private matter but a cosmic event.
27: vehefitz etchem YHWH baamim venishartem met'ei mispar baggoyim asher yeneheg YHWH etchem shammah: 'The LORD will scatter you among the peoples, and you will be left few in number among the nations where the LORD drives you.' Vehefitz etchem (he will scatter you) — the root putz (to scatter, disperse) is the exile vocabulary. Moses's prediction of the Diaspora anticipates the Assyrian and Babylonian deportations (2 Kings 17:6; Deuteronomy 25:11) with precision. Gibbur YHWH — YHWH himself as the agent of exile. This is the covenant's own sanction: YHWH does not abandon his people when they are exiled; he is the one who exiles them, retaining sovereignty even in judgment.
28: va'avadtem sham elohim ma'asei yedei adam etz va'even asher lo-yir'un velo yishme'un velo yokhelu velo yerihun: 'And there you will serve gods made by human hands — wood and stone — that can neither see, nor hear, nor eat, nor smell.' The idol-polemic (wood-and-stone gods that lack all sense and life) is a characteristic prophetic refrain (Psalms 115:4-7; Deuteronomy 135:15-17; Isaiah 44:9-20; Jeremiah 10:3-5). Deuteronomy's irony is sharp: Israel, who heard YHWH speak out of fire (Deuteronomy 4:12), will end up worshipping objects that cannot even smell. The contrast between the living, speaking, hearing YHWH and the senseless idols is Deuteronomy's ultimate anti-idolatry argument.
29: uviqashtem misham et-YHWH Eloheicha umatzata ki tidreshenu bekhol-levavcha uvkhol-nafshecha: 'But from there you will seek the LORD your God and you will find him, if you search for him with all your heart and with all your soul.' The restoration promise embedded in the exile prediction: uviqashtem... umatzata (you will seek and you will find). The condition — bekhol-levavcha uvkhol-nafshecha (with all your heart and all your soul) — is the positive formulation of the Shema's love command (Deuteronomy 6:5). YHWH is findable (matzata) precisely in exile, from the scattered condition — the locus of finding is not the temple or the land but the posture of whole-heart seeking. James 4:8 ('draw near to God and he will draw near to you') is a NT application of this promise.
30: batzar lekha umtza'uha kol-hadevarim ha'eleh be'aharit hayamim veshavta ad-YHWH Eloheicha: 'When you are in distress and all these things have come upon you, in the latter days you will return to the LORD your God.' Be'aharit hayamim (in the latter days) — a temporal phrase that in the prophets takes on an eschatological dimension: the 'latter days' are the time of YHWH's definitive act of restoration (Isaiah 2:2; Micah 4:1; Jeremiah 23:20; Deuteronomy 30:24). Moses here already anticipates not merely a historical return from exile but the eschatological return to YHWH that the prophets unfold. The NT presents this eschatological return as inaugurated in Christ's person and mission.
31: ki El Rachum YHWH Eloheicha lo yarpekha velo yashkhitekha velo yishkah et-berit avoteicha asher nishba lahem: 'For the LORD your God is a merciful God. He will not abandon you or destroy you or forget the covenant with your fathers that he swore to them.' El Rachum — YHWH as the compassionate/merciful God (rachum from rechem, womb) is the self-disclosure of Exodus 34:6-7, the great divine name-proclamation. The restoration after exile is grounded not in Israel's merit but in YHWH's covenantal faithfulness and compassion. The triple negation — lo yarpekha, lo yashkhitekha, lo yishkah (will not abandon, will not destroy, will not forget) — emphasizes the irreversibility of YHWH's covenantal commitment.
32: ki sh'al-na liyamim rishonim asher hayu lefanekha lemi'an hayom asher bara Elohim adam al-haaretz: 'For ask now about the former days, before you, since the day God created mankind on the earth.' The appeal to universal history — from creation to the present — as evidence for YHWH's uniqueness. Hayom asher bara Elohim adam (the day God created mankind) — the creation is the starting point for historical inquiry. Moses challenges his audience to survey all of human history and find a parallel to Horeb: has any other nation's god spoken from fire, or taken one nation out of another? The implied answer grounds Israelite monotheism not in abstract philosophy but in historical uniqueness.
33: heshama am qol Elohim medabber mitokh ha'esh ka'asher-shamata attah vayehi: 'Did any people ever hear the voice of God speaking out of the midst of fire, as you have heard, and survive?' Vayehi (and survive/live) — the astonishing fact is not just that Israel heard the divine voice but that they survived it. In the ancient Near East, direct encounter with the divine was universally assumed to be lethal; the survivor of a theophany is exceptional (cf. Gideon's fear in Judges 6:22-23; Isaiah's terror in Isaiah 6:5). Israel's corporate survival of Horeb is therefore evidence of YHWH's grace, not just his power.
34: o hanisoah Elohim lavo laqahat lo goy miqerev goy bemassot be'otot uvemoftim uvemilhamah ubeyyad hazaqah uv'izzroa netuyah uvmora'im gedolim kekhol asher-asah lachem YHWH Eloheichem beMitzrayim le'einekha: 'Or has any god ever attempted to go and take one nation out of the midst of another nation?' The Exodus is cited as the unique redemptive act in all of history: seven categories of divine action are listed (tests, signs, wonders, war, mighty hand, outstretched arm, great terrors) — the same combination of terms that becomes liturgical in the Passover Haggadah. The redemption of Israel from Egypt is the OT's defining act of salvation; the NT presents the cross-and-resurrection as the new Exodus that this event typified.
36: min-hashamayim hishmi'echa et-qolo leyasserecha uve'al haaretz her'echa et-isho haggedolah: 'Out of heaven he let you hear his voice to discipline you, and on earth he showed you his great fire.' The two modes of revelation — voice from heaven (min-hashamayim) and fire on earth (ue'al haaretz) — are distinguished: the voice disciplines (leyasserecha), the fire demonstrates holiness. Yasar (to discipline, instruct) is the word for fatherly moral education (Proverbs 3:11-12; Deuteronomy 19:18) — YHWH's voice at Horeb was not mere command but formative, parental instruction. Hebrews 12:18-21 uses the Horeb fire and voice as the counterpoint to the better access believers have through Christ.
37: vetahat ki ahav et-avotekha vayivchar bezaro aharav vayotziakha lefanav bekoho hagadol miMitzrayim: 'And because he loved your fathers and chose their descendants after them, he brought you out of Egypt with his great power.' The theological sequence is decisive: election precedes exodus. Ki ahav et-avotekha (because he loved your fathers) — the Exodus is a consequence of prior covenantal love, not a new initiative. Vayivchar bezaro aharav (and he chose his descendants after them) — Israel's election is carried in the bloodline of the patriarchs as a matter of covenantal continuity. Paul's treatment of election in Romans 9-11 engages directly with this Deuteronomic framework.
38: lehoresh goyim gedolim va'atzumim mimekha milfanekha lehavi'akha latet lekha et-artzam nachala kayom hazeh: 'To drive out before you nations greater and mightier than you, to bring you in and give you their land as an inheritance, as it is today.' Nachala (inheritance) — the land is not conquered property but covenantal inheritance, a gift that YHWH gives from his sovereign ownership. The phrase kayom hazeh (as it is today) grounds the theological claim in observable political reality: the land-gift is not a future hope but a present fact. The NT uses the same inheritance language for the spiritual blessings of the new covenant (Ephesians 1:11,14,18: 'the riches of his glorious inheritance').
39: veyadata hayom vahashevota el-levavecha ki YHWH hu ha-Elohim bashamayim mima'al ve'al-ha'aretz mitahat ein od: 'Know therefore today, and take it to heart, that the LORD is God in heaven above and on the earth beneath; there is no other.' The formula YHWH hu ha-Elohim (YHWH — he is God) appears most dramatically at Mount Carmel, where the people respond to Elijah's victory over Baal's prophets with the same confession (1 Kings 18:39). Bashamayim mima'al ve'al-ha'aretz mitahat (in heaven above and on earth beneath) — the comprehensive sovereignty covers the entire cosmological vertical axis. The double-directional claim rules out any local or partial deity: YHWH is God of the entire created cosmos.
40: veshomarta et-huqqav ve'et-mitzvotav asher anokhi metzavkha hayom asher yitav lekha velvanekha aharekha: 'Therefore keep his statutes and commandments that I am giving you today, so that it may go well with you and your children after you.' The concluding exhortation of the theological section (Deuteronomy 4:1-40) brings the argument full circle: from imperatives to history to theology and back to imperatives. Asher yitav lekha (that it may go well with you) — the covenant's functional logic: obedience generates flourishing, not as mechanical cause-and-effect but as covenantal alignment between a people and the God who designed them. The phrase recurs throughout Deuteronomy as the motivational signature of the covenant.
41: az yavdil Moshe shalosh arim be'ever haYarden mizrahah shamesh: 'Then Moses set apart three cities in the east beyond the Jordan.' The legal provision for cities of refuge (Numbers 35; Deut 19) is here implemented for the Transjordanian territory. Yavdil (set apart) — the same verb used for separating the sacred from the profane (havdil in Leviticus 10:10; the Havdalah ceremony). The cities of refuge are a covenantal institution that protects the inadvertent killer from blood vengeance. Their theological function is to preserve the purity of the land by distinguishing murder from manslaughter.
42: lanus shammah rotzeah asher yirzah et-re'ahu bildi da'at: 'So that a manslayer could flee there — anyone who kills his neighbor unintentionally without prior enmity.' The legal definition — bildi da'at (without knowledge/intent) — distinguishes accidental homicide from premeditated murder. The cities of refuge embody a principle of legal mercy: intentionality matters in YHWH's justice. This principle runs through the NT (the parable of the talents presupposes degrees of culpability; Jesus's distinction in John 9:41 — 'if you were blind you would have no sin') and shapes Christian ethics' treatment of motive alongside act.
43: Betzer bamidbar bearetz hamishor laRe'uveni veRamot beGil'ad lagGadi veGolan baBashan laMenuasshi: 'Bezer in the wilderness on the tableland for the Reubenites; Ramoth in Gilead for the Gadites; and Golan in Bashan for the Manassites.' The three named cities correspond to the three Transjordanian tribal territories. Bezer, Ramoth, and Golan form a geographic distribution that ensures no Transjordanian manslayer is too far from refuge. Ramoth-Gilead becomes significant in Israel's later history (1 Kgs 22; 2 Kgs 8-9) as a contested border city with Aram — its role as a city of refuge makes its later military loss particularly symbolic.
44: vezot haTorah asher-sam Moshe lifnei benei Yisra'el: 'This is the law that Moses set before the people of Israel.' The colophon-formula introduces what follows (Deuteronomy 4:44-49) as the superscription to the main Torah speeches of Deuteronomy 5-26. Zot haTorah (this is the Torah) — the demonstrative zot (this) points forward to the entire body of instruction. The formula is liturgical: the same zot haTorah is used in the synagogue when the Torah is lifted after reading. The written Torah as a defined, demarcated body of instruction is Deuteronomy's great contribution to Israelite religion.
45: eleh ha'edot vehahuqqim vehamishpatim asher dibber Moshe el-benei Yisra'el betzetam miMitzrayim: 'These are the testimonies, the statutes, and the rules that Moses spoke to the Israelites after they came out of Egypt.' The three-term typology — edot (testimonies, covenant ordinances), huqqim (fixed statutes), mishpatim (case-law rules) — is Deuteronomy's standard legal taxonomy, also found in Ps 119 where the three terms appear throughout the alphabetic meditation on Torah. The geographic tag (betzetam miMitzrayim, after their coming out of Egypt) situates all of Torah in the Exodus context: the law is given to a redeemed people, not as a mechanism of earning redemption.
46: be'ever haYarden beghai mul Beit-Pe'or be'eretz Sihon melekh haEmori asher yoshev beHeshbon: 'East of the Jordan in the valley opposite Beth-peor, in the land of Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived at Heshbon.' The geographic specification grounds the entire legal corpus in a historical moment: Torah was given in a specific place (Beth-peor valley), in a specific political context (the just-conquered land of Sihon). Law is not abstract; it is given to a particular people in a particular historical situation that shapes its application.
47: vayirshu et-artzo ve'et-aretz Og melekh-haBashan shney malkhei haEmori asher be'ever haYarden mizrah-shemesh: 'They had taken possession of his land and the land of Og king of Bashan — the two Amorite kings east of the Jordan.' The paired conquest — Sihon and Og — is cited again as the territorial context for the following Torah speeches. The repetition is not careless; it serves the document's legal function of establishing the standing of the recipient people: they are a people who have received YHWH's promises, received YHWH's word, and already experienced YHWH's faithfulness in battle.
48: me'Aro'er asher al-sfat nahal Arnon ve'ad-Har Siyyon hu Hermon: 'From Aroer on the rim of the Valley of the Arnon as far as Mount Sirion — that is, Hermon.' The parenthetical identification — hu Hermon (that is, Hermon) — clarifies that the Amorite name Sirion (Deuteronomy 3:9) refers to the same peak as Hermon. The geographic precision defines the full extent of Transjordanian Israel from the Arnon (south) to Hermon (north) — the entire territory that sets the stage for the covenant-giving speeches.
49: vekhol-ha'Aravah ever haYarden mizrahah ve'ad yam ha'Aravah tahat ashdot haPisga: 'Along with all the Arabah east of the Jordan as far as the Sea of the Arabah, under the slopes of Pisgah.' The Arabah, the Jordan Rift Valley, and Pisgah frame the setting of Deuteronomy's speeches. Pisgah is the site from which Moses will view the promised land before his death (Deuteronomy 34:1) — by mentioning it in the geographic frame of Deuteronomy 4, the narrator foreshadows Moses's end. The entire legal section of Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 5-30+) is thus delivered in the shadow of Moses's impending death — a last testament given from the edge of the promised land.
